reasoning: We saw the 7-year yield fall briefly from 4.25% in late March to 4.10% by April 9. However, the subsequent print on Friday April 10 (the closest trading day effectively setting the April 12 baseline) saw the 10-year rally 6 bps, suggesting the 7-year anchored slightly higher around 4.15-4.16%. The prevailing macro market narrative currently emphasizes sticky inflation, delayed rate cuts, massive Treasury issuance to fund deficits, and a rising term premium. Consensus expectations in early April 2026 project short-duration yields to eventually edge down as the Fed eases, while long-duration and medium-duration (7-to-10 year belly) bonds grind higher or hold steady due to supply. Thus, momentum and structural macro trends slightly favor an increase sequentially over the 1-to-6 month horizons. Over a multi-year horizon, as inflation neutralizes and economic cycles reset, certainty decays, bringing the longer-term forecasts straight back to a balanced 0.50.
